content:
Hi, I am working on a I2C driver that will also use GPIO bindings that I am hoping to upstream soon along with my own original bindings. That said, I am curious how people deal with suppressing warning type errors while doing developments. Things like "missing documentation for a module". I can easily add the attribute everywhere I need it, but I presume we should have some Kconfig option for this in general, similar to CONFIG_WERROR.

error: missing documentation for a module
  --> rust/kernel/lib.rs:38:1
   |
38 | pub mod i2c;
   | ^^^^^^^^^^^
   |
   = note: `-D missing-docs` implied by `-D warnings`
   = help: to override `-D warnings` add `#[allow(missing_docs)]`

For now, this works for me but was curious if there is anything better.

make ARCH=arm64 CROSS_COMPILE=aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu- KRUSTFLAGS='-A warnings' -j $(nproc) Image modules dtbs
Dosen't WERROR=n work? At least my builds do not stop due to missing docs.

Yeah, I have CONFIG_WERROR unset.

linux on  adafruit-joy-bonnet [?] via ❄️  impure (nix-shell-aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu) took 23s
❯ rg WERROR .config
30:# CONFIG_WERROR is not set
So warnings in C kernel modules are not escalated to errors for me, but I do not see the same for the Rust code I am developing.
Something is amiss, we only set -Dwarnings under WERROR. Which tree are you using?
I forked the rust-for-linux tree rust-next branch at commit a2f115470520 ("kasan: rust: Add KASAN smoke test via UAF")
Back then that was still true. Are you setting some relevant env vars by chance?
e.g. can you try in a "clean" environment? I see a Nix shell there, could that have some customizations?

Yeah, I dumped env just to be safe. Nothing of interest there. Double checked my nix-shell and Nix's rustc wrappings (though I could have missed something). I can try a clean environment as well as debug what is happening and where is that flag propagating from. Will post back with my findings.

When I did a clean build, the errors downgraded to warnings. I would like to try reproducing the problem though because I think the build system should be tolerant to this. My theory is the following.

  1. WERROR=y and then ran into -Dwarnings
  2. Changed my config to unset WERROR
  3. -Dwarnings was still propagated for some reason.

I am going to look into that a bit more to see if any cleanup is needed on the build side.
Hmmm, cannot reproduce the issue trivially. For now, I can leave it alone. If I stumble into it again, I will try to properly debug just to confirm the root cause.

Just so you know there is an I2C abstraction here: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commit/b087b606a57b3047ef0c8c8d79f3fdffd2fff6e2
As well as a beginning of GPIOD consumer abstraction over here: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commit/a566fbf03084c7ac1b493134381ba2ab70af3e9d

There is also this one for I2C which is based on newer patch series from the ML but hasn't been tested yet (will do it once I come back from my travel): https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commit/441299e4e7621d232f193fe072fcd04204ae2059 . It will be sent to the ML soon.
Do you have a consumer for your abstractions already as well? I can try basing my driver on top of your abstractions instead.

I have two drivers that I plan to send soon:

  • https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commits/fparent/rust-ncv6336/: This branch contains abstractions for I2C, Regmap, Regulator consumer and driver. The driver itself is over here: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commit/33ca6cd8483142149a72025e566943c93e99d1c2
  • https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commits/fparent/rust-truly-r63350/: this branch contains abstractions for GPIOD consumer, MIPI DSI, DRM Panels. The driver itself is here: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commit/7dcbfa7cc508a2d871e2ad1ccfa4adc25261526f

The two branches above have been tested and are working.

I'm currently cleaning everything and fixing a few things here in order to submit both branches, but the work is being done over here:

  • https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commits/b4/ncv6336/
    These are not tested at all since I'm working on it while traveling and will be able to test it only when I come home.

No rush. I can wait till you are more finalized. Is there a thread you know for coordinating bindings and driver development? I also wanted to work on a Intel HDA quirk driver for supporting the Creative SoundBlaster AE-9, but I am worried about running into collisions with other people's work.

I would suggest to search the following resources before starting to work on new abstractions:

  • https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/
  • https://github.com/tgross35/RFL-patch-registry
  • This zulip instance

I'm not aware of anyone working on audio abstractions
